summary: DeVault argues that free software projects have an ethical obligation to use free software infrastructure rather than proprietary platforms like GitHub, Discord, or Twitter. He contends that choosing proprietary platforms lends them legitimacy and network effects at the direct expense of free alternatives like Codeberg, Matrix, and Mastodon. The asymmetry of network effects means that each project's choice disproportionately hurts or helps the smaller FOSS platforms. He dismisses the argument that proprietary platforms offer superior access to audiences as selfish and short-term thinking, noting that many FOSS platforms are functionally superior and offer the additional advantage of collective ownership. The post concludes with concrete recommendations for free alternatives across code hosting, messaging, publishing, and social media.
key_points: Choosing a proprietary platform is an official vote of confidence that invests in its network effects while divesting from free alternatives
The asymmetry of network effects means losing a project hurts Codeberg far more than it hurts GitHub, making each project's choice disproportionately impactful
Projects that choose proprietary platforms for popularity are making a selfish, individualist, short-term investment at the expense of the FOSS ecosystem
FOSS platforms offer a unique advantage: if something doesn't work for your project, you can fix it yourself rather than waiting on a commercial entity's priorities
Ethical objections to platform owners (e.g., GitHub's ICE collaboration) are easily rationalized away through confirmation bias by projects that don't want to move
money_quotes: An investment in a non-free platform is also a divestment from the free alternatives.
Your choice matters disproportionately to the free platforms: choosing GitHub hurts Codeberg much more than choosing Codeberg hurts GitHub.
Why should a project choose to use your offering over the proprietary alternatives if you won't extend them the same courtesy?
Choosing non-free platforms is an individualist, short-term investment which prioritizes your project's apparent access to popularity over the success of the FOSS ecosystem as a whole.
If a problem is important to you, that's enough for you to get it fixed on a FOSS platform.
These projects are not ghettos living in the shadow of their larger commercial counterparts, but exciting platforms in their own right which offer many unique advantages.
The easiest time to revisit this choice is right now. It will only ever get more difficult to move as your project grows.
key_insight: Every FOSS project's infrastructure choice is a vote that either strengthens or undermines the free software ecosystem, and the asymmetry of network effects makes choosing proprietary platforms disproportionately harmful to free alternatives.
